name: exec
context: Run commands inside running Docker containers for debugging, administration, and one-off tasks
info: This sub-skill covers docker exec for executing commands inside running containers. docker exec starts a new process in a running container's namespace, sharing its filesystem, network, and environment. Common uses include opening a shell for debugging, running database migrations, checking service health, tailing logs inside the container, inspecting the filesystem, and executing administrative scripts. Use -it for interactive terminal access. Use -d for detached (background) execution. Use -u to run as a specific user. Use -w to set the working directory. Use -e to pass additional environment variables. The exec command does not modify the container image or create new layers. This sub-skill focuses on exec operations; see [[docker/containers/run]] for starting containers and [[docker/containers/logs]] for viewing output.
triggers: run a command in a running container
body:
# Executing Commands in Containers

`docker exec` runs a new process inside an already-running container. The process shares the container's PID namespace, network, filesystem, and environment. It does not create new image layers or modify the container image.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Run a command and see output
docker exec myapp cat /etc/hostname

# Open an interactive shell
docker exec -it myapp /bin/sh
docker exec -it myapp /bin/bash

# Run in detached mode (background)
docker exec -d myapp touch /tmp/healthcheck
```

## Interactive Shells

The most common use of exec is opening a shell inside a container for debugging:

```bash
# Alpine/minimal images (sh only)
docker exec -it myapp /bin/sh

# Ubuntu/Debian images (bash available)
docker exec -it myapp /bin/bash

# Check which shells are available
docker exec myapp cat /etc/shells

# If no shell is available (distroless/scratch), use debug container:
docker debug myapp  # Docker 25+ with debug images
```

The `-i` flag keeps STDIN open, and `-t` allocates a pseudo-TTY. Both are needed for an interactive terminal session.

## Running as a Specific User

```bash
# Run as root (even if container runs as non-root)
docker exec -u root myapp apt-get update

# Run as a specific user
docker exec -u node myapp npm run migrate

# Run as UID:GID
docker exec -u 1000:1000 myapp whoami
```

This is useful when the container runs as a non-root user but you need root for debugging or administration.

## Setting Working Directory

```bash
# Execute from a specific directory
docker exec -w /app myapp ls -la
docker exec -w /app/src myapp npm test
docker exec -w /var/log myapp tail -f app.log
```

## Passing Environment Variables

```bash
# Pass additional env vars for the exec session
docker exec -e DEBUG=true -e LOG_LEVEL=verbose myapp node debug-script.js

# Run migration with database URL override
docker exec -e DATABASE_URL=postgres://admin:pass@db:5432/myapp myapp npm run migrate
```

Note: environment variables passed to exec are only available to that specific exec process, not to the main container process.

## Database Operations

```bash
# PostgreSQL
docker exec -it postgres psql -U app -d myapp
docker exec postgres pg_dump -U app myapp > backup.sql

# MySQL
docker exec -it mysql mysql -u root -p
docker exec mysql mysqldump -u root -p myapp > backup.sql

# Redis
docker exec -it redis redis-cli
docker exec redis redis-cli INFO memory

# MongoDB
docker exec -it mongo mongosh
docker exec mongo mongodump --out /dump
```

## Debugging Patterns

```bash
# Check processes running inside
docker exec myapp ps aux

# Check network configuration
docker exec myapp ip addr
docker exec myapp cat /etc/resolv.conf
docker exec myapp nslookup database

# Check filesystem
docker exec myapp df -h
docker exec myapp ls -la /app
docker exec myapp find /app -name '*.log' -size +10M

# Check environment
docker exec myapp env
docker exec myapp printenv DATABASE_URL

# Check connectivity
docker exec myapp curl -s http://localhost:3000/health
docker exec myapp wget -qO- http://api:8080/status

# Install debugging tools (in non-production!)
docker exec -u root myapp apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl net-tools strace
docker exec -u root myapp apk add --no-cache curl bind-tools
```

## Script Execution

```bash
# Run a script already inside the container
docker exec myapp /app/scripts/migrate.sh

# Pipe a script from host into container
docker exec -i myapp /bin/sh < local-script.sh

# Run inline multi-command script
docker exec myapp /bin/sh -c 'cd /app && npm run migrate && npm run seed'

# Copy a file in, then execute it
docker cp script.sh myapp:/tmp/script.sh
docker exec myapp chmod +x /tmp/script.sh
docker exec myapp /tmp/script.sh
```

## Health Checking

```bash
# HTTP health check
docker exec myapp curl -sf http://localhost:3000/health || echo 'UNHEALTHY'

# Process check
docker exec myapp pgrep -f 'node server.js' || echo 'PROCESS NOT RUNNING'

# File-based health check
docker exec myapp test -f /tmp/healthy && echo 'HEALTHY' || echo 'UNHEALTHY'

# Database connectivity check
docker exec myapp pg_isready -h localhost -U app || echo 'DB NOT READY'
```

## Best Practices

- Use `--user` to avoid running exec as root when possible
- Prefer running migrations and admin tasks as ephemeral containers (`docker run --rm`) rather than exec
- Do not install packages in production containers via exec -- rebuild the image instead
- Use exec for debugging, not for regular application operations
- Remember that exec processes are not restarted if they crash -- only the main container process respects restart policies

sections.title: Interactive shells
sections.body: Open a shell inside a container with docker exec -it <container> /bin/sh (or /bin/bash if available). The -i flag keeps STDIN open, -t allocates a TTY. For distroless or scratch images with no shell, use docker debug (Docker 25+). Check available shells with cat /etc/shells.

sections.title: User context and permissions
sections.body: Use -u to override the exec user. Run as root for administration even if the container runs as non-root. Run as a specific UID:GID for permission-sensitive operations. The user context affects filesystem access, process ownership, and capability restrictions.

sections.title: Database operations
sections.body: Exec into database containers for administration: psql for PostgreSQL, mysql for MySQL, redis-cli for Redis, mongosh for MongoDB. Run backups with pg_dump, mysqldump, or mongodump. Pipe output to host for backup storage. Use exec for interactive queries and one-off administrative commands.

sections.title: Debugging patterns
sections.body: Inspect processes (ps aux), network config (ip addr, nslookup), filesystem (ls, df, find), environment (env, printenv), and connectivity (curl, wget) inside the container. Install debug tools temporarily in non-production containers. For production, use docker debug or ephemeral debug containers on the same network.

sections.title: Exec best practices
sections.body: Use exec for debugging and one-off tasks, not regular operations. Prefer ephemeral containers (docker run --rm) for migrations and administrative scripts. Do not install packages in production containers via exec -- rebuild the image. Exec processes are not covered by restart policies. Use --user to follow least-privilege principles.

links.context: Parent skill for container lifecycle and management
Start containers before exec-ing into them
View container output; complementary to exec for debugging
Debug network connectivity from inside containers

related.note: Containers must be running before exec can be used
Logs show stdout/stderr; exec provides interactive access
docker compose exec provides the same functionality for Compose services

scripts.name: Exec Shell
scripts.description: Open an interactive shell inside a running Docker container
scripts.inline: SHELL_CMD="${SHELL_CMD:-/bin/sh}" && USER_FLAG="${USER_ID:+--user $USER_ID}" && WORKDIR_FLAG="${WORKDIR:+-w $WORKDIR}" && echo "Opening shell in container '${CONTAINER}'..." && docker exec -it $USER_FLAG $WORKDIR_FLAG "$CONTAINER" "$SHELL_CMD"

scripts.name: Exec Command
scripts.description: Execute a command inside a running Docker container and return the output
scripts.inline: echo "Executing in container '${CONTAINER}': ${COMMAND}" && echo '---' && docker exec ${USER_ID:+--user "$USER_ID"} ${WORKDIR:+-w "$WORKDIR"} ${ENV_VARS:+$(echo "$ENV_VARS" | tr ',' '\n' | sed 's/^/-e /')} "$CONTAINER" /bin/sh -c "$COMMAND" && echo '' && echo '--- Exit code: 0'

scripts.name: Debug Container
scripts.description: Run a comprehensive diagnostic check inside a running container
scripts.inline: echo "=== Container: ${CONTAINER} ===" && echo '' && echo '--- Hostname ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" hostname 2>/dev/null && echo '' && echo '--- Processes ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" ps aux 2>/dev/null || docker exec "$CONTAINER" ls /proc 2>/dev/null && echo '' && echo '--- Environment ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" env 2>/dev/null | sort && echo '' && echo '--- Filesystem ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" df -h 2>/dev/null && echo '' && echo '--- Network ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" cat /etc/resolv.conf 2>/dev/null && echo '' && if [ -n "$HEALTH_URL" ]; then echo '--- Health Check ---' && docker exec "$CONTAINER" wget -qO- "$HEALTH_URL" 2>/dev/null || docker exec "$CONTAINER" curl -sf "$HEALTH_URL" 2>/dev/null || echo 'Health check failed (no curl/wget)'; fi

CONTAINER.description: Container name or ID to debug

HEALTH_URL.description: Health check URL to test (e.g. http://localhost:3000/health)

command.description: Command to execute inside the container

user.description: User to execute as (name or UID:GID)

environment.description: Additional environment variables for the exec process

depends.reason: Docker Engine must be installed and running
Container must be running before exec

author: dojo-community
